codeReview.summary: Clean, well-structured implementation of drop shadows beneath chrome elements. The drawing library gains a general-purpose `fill_gradient_v()` method using integer-only linear interpolation with proper bounds checking and clipping. The compositor adds two shadow surfaces at z=15 (between content z=10 and chrome z=20) — title shadow below the title bar and status shadow above the status bar. Both use 8-pixel gradient falloff from alpha 80→0. Shadow surfaces are rendered once at startup and included in both the initial and event-loop composite passes. The implementation correctly handles both gradient directions (top-down for title shadow, bottom-up for status shadow). 10 well-targeted tests cover boundary values, monotonicity, column uniformity, clipping, edge cases, z-order integration, and gradient smoothness verification.

issues.description: Potential u32 overflow in fill_gradient_v interpolation for very large surface heights. The computation `color_top.r as u32 * (denom - t)` where denom=(h-1) can overflow u32 when h > ~16.8M pixels (255 * 16_777_216 = 4_278_190_080 > u32::MAX). Not triggered by any current usage (SHADOW_DEPTH=8, max framebuffer ~1920x1080), but the method is pub and general-purpose. Could be addressed by using u64 intermediates if the method ever needs to handle large gradients.

sharedStateObservations.observation: `.factory/library/architecture.md` line 68 states 'No rounded rects, no blur, no gradients yet' under the Drawing Library section. This feature adds `fill_gradient_v()`, making the 'no gradients' part factually incorrect. The architecture knowledge base should be updated to reflect the new gradient capability.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: .factory/library/architecture.md:68 — 'No rounded rects, no blur, no gradients yet' vs system/libraries/drawing/lib.rs:618 — pub fn fill_gradient_v()

summary: Pass. The drop-shadows feature is correctly implemented with clean code, proper z-ordering, gradient falloff in both directions, and thorough test coverage. One non-blocking note: the fill_gradient_v interpolation uses u32 arithmetic that could theoretically overflow for surface heights above ~16.8M pixels — not reachable in practice but worth noting since the method is public. The architecture.md knowledge file was not updated to reflect the new gradient capability.
